name: Update User
description: The `update` (`db.update`) node's `error` output is wired to `respond_conflict`, mirroring register's duplicate-email envelope/status (422, 'email or username has already been taken'); db.update has only a single generic `error` output (no `exists`-style discriminator like auth.create_user), so any database error on this node — not just a unique-constraint violation — surfaces that message. `skip_password` is a deliberate no-op (`transform.set`) inserted on the has_password 'else' branch so both branches reach `update` through a proper node rather than one edge coming directly from the conditional itself: with a bare `has_password --else--> update` edge alongside `has_password --then--> set_password --> update`, the compiler's join-type heuristic (internal/engine/compiler.go computeJoinTypes) misclassified `update` as an AND-join (it only recognizes OR-joins when a common conditional ancestor is reached through the join sources via two distinct *reachable* outputs, and a self-edge from the conditional isn't a reachable descendant of itself) — an AND-join never fires because only one of the two mutually-exclusive branches executes per request, so the workflow hung/errored on every 'no password change' request. This was a real pre-existing defect (unrelated to the error-envelope fix) caught only because Task 8 added the first tests/update-user.json; documented here rather than filed as a separate engine issue since the workflow-level fix is a one-node, zero-behavior-change workaround.

body: email or username has already been taken
